tag_list: Califonia Burclover, Medicago polymorpha, Santa Clara County, Sunnyvale, Toothed Burclover, Toothed Medick
description:
California Burclover (Medicago polymorpha), also known as Toothed Burclover and Toothed Medick.  Photographed along El Camino Real, near the southern terminus of Acalanes Drive in Sunnyvale, CA.  Identified as California Burclover based on the following characteristics (per Jepson's eFlora):

1) From this plant's bilaterally symmetrical, pea-like flowers and compound leaves, one can quickly placed it in the Legume Family (Fabacaeae), a large complex of species that includes peas, lupines, and clovers. 
2) Now note that the upper petal (banner) appeared outside of the lower petals in bud. (See the cluster of unopened flowers to the right of the cluster of open ones.)
3) Leaf 1-pinnate, palmate, or simple. [In this case, the leaf is actually 1-pinnate. This gets me to the Papilionideae group within the Fabaceae family.]
4) Plant is an annual (not a shrub or a tree).
5) Each leaf consists of 3 leaflets, with the main leaf axis terminating in a leaflet. At first, the three leaflets makes it seem as if the leaf is palmately compound.  However, when you study the main axis of the leaf, it becomes apparent that the leaf is actually pinnately compound.
6) Lower petals are not spirally coiled.
7) Leaflet margin is toothed and wavy. 
8) Corolla yellow, ~4mm long.  This gets me to two genera - Melilotus and Medicago.  Jepson's distinguishes between these two genera based on characteristics of their seeds, which were not available at the time that this photograph was taken (late March).  However, plants belonging to the genus Melilotus have inflorescences that are many-flowered racemes, while the inflorescence in the photograph is a fairly compact, few-flowered raceme.  This inflorescence is consistent with the genus Medicago, nine species of which can be found in the state of California (all non-native).
9) The 4 mm long corolla eliminates Medicago lupulin from consideration.
10) The plant being a low-growing annual with a decumbent habit eliminates Medicago sativa from consideration.
11) From here, the remaining California species of the genus Medicago are differentiated based on wheter or not their seeds are prickly.  Of the three species whose seeds are not prickly. only one can be foune in the San Francisco Bay Area, M. orbicularis.  For that species, the stipules are dissected to the base, creating a cluster of bristles around the leaf axils.  The stipules of the species in the photograph are deeply toothed, but not dissected in that way, so it cannot be M. orbicularis.  By implication, this also means that the fruit of the species in the photograph are prickly.
12) The toothed stipules and glabrous nature of the herbage in the photograph eliminates two more species from the running (M. minima and M. rigidula).
13) The leaflets lacking a dark central spot eliminates M. arabica and M. truncatula from consideration.
14) Leaflets varying between 10 mm and 20 mm in length; corolla ~4 mm in length; and plant widespread and common. This final combination of characteristics identify the species in the photograph as the California Burclover (Medicago polymorpha), not the Mediterranean Medick (M. praecox), which was the last remaining contender.

A female. I have seen this bee and the species foraging exclusively on Claytonia perforata on which this bee is very quick and efficient at each flower.  Wallace LaBerge (1985) lists floral records (Anagallis sp., Claytonia spathulata, Medicago sativa, Claytonia sp., Claytonia. perforiata, Nemophila sp., Stelleria media).  

Stelleria media is flowering along with Claytonia perforata in the sites I have seen this bee, but it does not visit S. media so far. I think I've seen this species visit Nemophila heterophylla last year.

Females and males stopped to sun on leaves periodically if the temperature dropped.

Males pursue females actively, or sit and wait and ambush foraging females.

description:
Yellow, medium-size butterfly on Acmispon (Deerweed).

Orange Sulfur (Colias eurytheme) is a yellow, medium-size butterfly with large green eyes in the Whites, Yellows, and Sulphurs (Pieridae) family with 1.0-1.5 inch wingspan. They have 1 flight per year, February-November. Orange Sulfurs can be found on grassy slopes and are especially abundant in Alfalfa fields. They always sit with wings closed. Hindwing has a white dot with a pink margin.

Link to my favorite Orange Sulfur observation showing the prominent green eyes: https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/194590295

Butterflies of Monterey County: A comprehensive guide to finding and identifying 91 species of butterflies in Monterey County, C. Tenney, 2023, pp. 34-35.

Law's Field Guide to the Sierra Nevada, John Muir Laws, California Academy of Sciences, 2007, p.167.

Butterflies Through Binoculars: The West: a field guide to the butterflies of western North America, by Jeffrey Glassberg, 2001, pp. 60-61.

Bug photographs from the U.S/Canada for I.D: https://bugguide.net/index.php?q=search&keys=Colias%20eurytheme

Orange Sulphur (Colias eurytheme)
Butterflies and Moths of North America https://www.butterfliesandmoths.org/species/Colias-eurytheme
"Wing span is 1 -1.5 inches. Identification: Quite variable. Upperside of male yellow with orange overlay, yellow veins, wide black border, and dark black cell spot. Female yellow or white with irregular black border surrounding light spots. Underside hindwing spot silver with 2 concentric dark rings, and a spot above it. Caterpillar Hosts: Plants in the pea family (Fabaceae) including alfalfa (Medicago sativa), white clover (Trifolium repens), and white sweet clover (Melilotus alba). Adult Food: many kinds of flowers including dandelion, milkweeds, goldenrods, and asters. Habitat: A wide variety of open sites, especially clover and alfalfa fields, mowed fields, vacant lots, meadows, road edges."

Butterflies and Moths of North America https://www.butterfliesandmoths.org/species/Colias-eurytheme

description:
Orange Sulfur butterfly resting on Deerweed (Acmispon glaber) on a grassy trail next to a year-round stream.

Orange Sulfur (Colias eurytheme) is a yellow, medium-size butterfly with large green eyes in the Whites, Yellows, and Sulphurs (Pieridae) family with 1.0-1.5 inch wingspan. They have 1 flight per year, February-November. Orange Sulfurs can be found on grassy slopes and are especially abundant in Alfalfa fields. They always sit with wings closed. Hindwing has a white dot with a pink margin.

Link to my favorite Orange Sulfur observation showing the prominent green eyes: https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/194590295

"Wing span is 1 -1.5 inches. Identification: Quite variable. Upperside of male yellow with orange overlay, yellow veins, wide black border, and dark black cell spot. Female yellow or white with irregular black border surrounding light spots. Underside hindwing spot silver with 2 concentric dark rings, and a spot above it. Caterpillar Hosts: Plants in the pea family (Fabaceae) including alfalfa (Medicago sativa), white clover (Trifolium repens), and white sweet clover (Melilotus alba). Adult Food: many kinds of flowers including dandelion, milkweeds, goldenrods, and asters. Habitat: A wide variety of open sites, especially clover and alfalfa fields, mowed fields, vacant lots, meadows, road edges."
